questions.question: What is the difference between interface and type in TypeScript?
questions.answer: Both define object shapes. Key differences: (1) Interfaces can be extended/merged — declare the same interface twice and they merge (useful for augmenting library types). Types cannot be reopened. (2) Types can represent anything: union types (string | number), intersection types (&), mapped types, conditional types. Interfaces only describe object shapes. Convention: use interface for object types and public APIs (supports extension), use type for unions, intersections, primitives, and complex type operations.

questions.question: What is the "unknown" type and how is it different from "any"?
questions.answer: any disables TypeScript's type checking — you can call any method on it, assign it anywhere, TypeScript won't complain. It defeats the purpose of TypeScript. unknown is type-safe: you can assign anything to unknown, but you cannot use it until you narrow its type (with typeof, instanceof, or a type guard). unknown is the type-safe alternative to any for handling values of unknown origin (API responses, error objects). Always prefer unknown over any when you don't know the type.

questions.question: What are generics in TypeScript?
questions.answer: Generics allow you to write code that works with multiple types while preserving type information. Like a template: function first<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined returns the same type as what's in the array. Without generics: first(arr) returns any (loses type info). With generics: first(["a","b"]) returns string. Use generics for: data structures (Stack<T>, Queue<T>), API response wrappers (APIResponse<T>), utility functions that should work on any type while preserving type safety.

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency Template Literal Types queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
